5 min read
Just now

--

He had presented to his company’s executives that same morning. Slides, numbers, a room full of senior people, no problem. By the afternoon he was sitting in front of a black window full of white text, in our first session, and he had gone completely quiet.
We were installing a tool. I gave him a command, he typed it, pressed enter, and the screen answered with the two words that have ended more learning journeys than any hard concept ever has: command not found.
I watched his face do the thing I have now seen dozens of times. A small collapse. The quiet, certain thought arriving behind his eyes: I am not the kind of person who can do this. He laughed it off, a little too quickly, and said maybe this whole thing was not for him.
It was a trivial setup error. A path the installer had not registered yet. The fix takes about two minutes: close the window, correct one line, open it again. I have seen it a hundred times and so has every developer alive.
But the fix was not the point. The fear was.
What was actually in the room
Nothing was wrong with him and nothing was really wrong with the computer. What was in the room was years of a quiet belief that anything which looks like that window, terminals, code, the blinking cursor, belongs to other people. Smarter people. Younger people. People who are not him.
This is a man who can read a profit-and-loss statement at a glance, run a team, and hold a boardroom. A blinking cursor undid all of it in four seconds. That is not a knowledge gap. That is a story, and the story is what I actually had to address before any tool would ever stick.
So I did the only thing that matters in that moment, which was not the fix. I narrated it. I told him this error is normal, that it happens to me most weeks, that it means precisely nothing about whether he can do this, and that we would see it again and shrug. I fixed it slowly, out loud, so he could see there was no magic in it. The white text stopped being a verdict and became a speed bump.
The terminal was never the hard part. Being alone with it was.
Why I see this most in the most capable people
Here is the part that took me years to notice. The fear of the terminal is not strongest in beginners who lack confidence generally. It is often strongest in accomplished professionals who are excellent at their actual jobs. The more competent someone is in their domain, the more jarring it feels to be a beginner at anything, and the louder the “I am not technical” story runs.
I see it constantly in product managers. They direct engineering teams, they own roadmaps, they make calls that move real money, and many of them feel quietly locked out of the very tools their engineers use every day. They are not trying to become engineers. They just do not want to be lost in their own product. I wrote about exactly that tension in whether product managers should still learn to code, and the honest answer is that most of them need far less than they fear and gain far more than they expect.
The cost of the story is the real problem. It is not that these professionals cannot learn the terminal. It is that the fear keeps capable, decision-making people on the sidelines of the most important shift in their field, watching from a distance, outsourcing understanding to whoever on the team seems comfortable with the black window. That distance compounds. Every month you stay on the sideline, the gap between you and the tools feels a little wider, when in truth it was always an afternoon away.
The way through is not bravery, it is sequence
You do not fix a fear like this by gritting your teeth and forcing yourself into the scariest room first. You fix it by starting where the friction is genuinely low, getting a real win, and letting confidence accumulate.
For most non-developers, that starting point is not the terminal at all. It is the friendlier tools that do real work without the intimidating screen, which is why I almost always start professionals there and only reach for the command line when the work truly calls for it. If you are weighing where to begin, the plain breakdown of which AI tool to actually use will keep you from walking through the scariest door for no reason. And when the terminal really is the right tool, the move is to make sure your first error happens with someone beside you, so it registers as “normal” instead of “not for me.”
This matters more now than it ever has, because the tools are not a niche anymore. By the World Economic Forum’s own Future of Jobs reporting, the work that touches AI is spreading into every profession, not just engineering. The people who let an old story keep them away from a black window are not avoiding discomfort. They are quietly opting out of the decade’s biggest skill shift, on the basis of a feeling that takes twenty minutes to disprove.
What he did next
He finished that first session asking when the next one was. Not because the terminal had become easy in an hour, but because it had become ordinary. The thing he had been afraid of turned out to be smaller than the fear that guarded it, which is almost always the case.
A few weeks later he was working in that same window without flinching, getting real things done, occasionally hitting an error and, by then, just fixing it. Nothing about his intelligence had changed. He was exactly as capable on the last day as the first. The only thing that changed was the story he told himself about who gets to use these tools.
That is the part I wish more accomplished professionals understood before they write themselves out of this. The fear is almost always bigger than the thing. The concept you are dreading usually takes an afternoon. The terminal takes twenty minutes with someone beside you. What takes years, if you let it, is the belief that you are not a technical person, and that belief is wrong often enough that I now watch it break almost every week.
If a black window full of white text has quietly been keeping you on the sideline, a free Discovery Call is a low-stakes way to find out how small the real obstacle is. Most people are genuinely surprised.
